text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic nascent fundoplication to repair a hiatus hernia, using minimally invasive techniques to wrap the upper part of the stomach around the lower esophagus.
The surgeon inserts a 12mm OptiView trocar into the abdominal cavity following the establishment of pneumoperitoneum, providing a clear view for laparoscopic access.
Good diagnostic laparoscopy.
Two 5mm ports are inserted as working ports, with an additional 5mm port placed on the left side for gastric retraction during the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon uses a three millimeter Nathanson retractor to elevate the liver, providing clear access to the surgical field for the hiatus hernia repair.

The patient is positioned head up, and the surgeon begins gastrolysis on the stomach, carefully dissecting the short gastric vessels up to the phrenoesophageal membrane while avoiding injury to the stomach and esophagus.

The surgeon opens the gastrohepatic ligament, also known as the Kushner membrane, to expose the right crus. Care is taken to avoid penetrating the red meaty fibers of the right crus, ensuring they remain covered by a layer of fat.
The surgeon dissects the Belsi pad of fat from the anterior aspect of the esophagus, working close to the crura.
The surgeon performs retro-oesophageal dissection, attempting to enter the thoracic cavity for better mobilization of the oesophagus.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection to meet the apex of the right and left crura, ensuring the meaty fibers of the left crus remain covered by a sheath. A twelve-inch piece of umbilical tape is passed through the created window.
The surgeon retracts the esophagus upward during the retroesophageal dissection, facilitating better exposure and mobilization of the surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to free or desiccate all attachments around the esophagus, ensuring the posterior vagus nerve remains close to the esophagus. After complete mobilization of the esophagus, crurography is performed using a polyester number two suture with a 45 mm taper point needle.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers the instrument tip to avoid injuring the liver, stomach, or esophagus during the procedure.
The surgeon places two to three sutures to close the crural defect, ensuring the anatomical structures are securely approximated.

But once we reach close to the esophagus, we strongly believe that it should not be over tightened because there are drugs which are available for the reflux disease, but there are no pills available for the obstructive features.
The surgeon checks the adequacy of the crural defect closure using a gastric calibration tube, ensuring proper alignment and closure of the defect.
The surgeon creates a window and begins forming a gastro-gastric wrap, manipulating the stomach tissue to wrap around itself.

The surgeon places a stay suture on the posterior wall of the stomach, positioned high on the fundus, leaving it long. The stomach is then maneuvered retroesophageal, avoiding retro gastric positioning.
The surgeon identifies the silk suture placed for closing the crural defect, ensuring its position and tension are appropriate.
The surgeon performs the shoe shine maneuver to ensure there is no twisting between the anterior and posterior walls of the stomach during the wrap formation.
The surgeon begins the gastro-gastric wrap using 2-0 polyester sutures, placing serosal sutures to secure the wrap around the stomach.
The surgeon includes the anterior wall of the esophagus in the first suture of the gastro-gastric wrap to prevent slippage, ensuring secure attachment of the wrap to the esophagus.
The surgeon secures the right side of the stomach to the right crus of the diaphragm for cruropexy, ensuring the wrap remains stable and does not migrate into the thorax post-operatively.
The surgeon completes the gastro-gastric suturing by placing three or four 2-0 polyester sutures, ensuring secure fixation of the stomach wrap.

The surgeon places gastro-gastric sutures to create a wrap approximately 3.5 centimeters in length, ensuring it is retro-esophageal rather than retro-gastric. The Nathanson port and other ports are removed under direct vision, and no drain is left in place.
